channel.title: The Quietus – Album of the Week
channel.description: Album of the Week reviews from The Quietus.

item.title: The Four Seasons: Bill Orcutt’s Music In Continuous Motion
item.description: The latest work for four guitars by the Miami-born musician and composer sounds like a history lesson in guitar technique to Bernie Brooks

item.title: Crimes of the Future: Torn by Cobrah
item.description: BDSM meets industrial beats on the new album from the sex-positive Swedish singer-producer

item.title: The Forest Murmurs: Marathon by Maria BC
item.description: Understated yet subtly devastating, the Oakland, California-baed artist turns down the aggression of previous album Spike Field in favour of a more tender kind of violence

item.title: Ancient to the Future: Discombobulated by Hen Ogledd
item.description: The Hen Ogledd family keeps on expanding – the group’s sound, likewise. But more than anything, Discombobulated sounds like the present, finds Jeanette Leech

item.title: Et In Arcadia Emo: The Apple Tree Under The Sea by Hemlocke Springs
item.description: The debut album from the North Carolina-born singer-songwriter-producer, finds the digital age's only medieval princess bursting with ideas and cartoonish intensity. Truly, Hemlocke Springs contains multitudes

item.title: Justice for All or Justice for None: Urgh by Mandy, Indiana
item.description: Manchester’s Mandy, Indiana haver never sounded so direct, so fierce, so angry as on this, their second album, a record which forcefully calls out rape culture and toxic masculinity amidst racing polyrhythms and a barrage of noise

item.title: Black to the Future: A Bleak King Cometh by Qasu
item.description: Even within a genre increasingly filled with aberrations, Qasu's debut represents an odd yet scintillating proposition

item.title: Exploding Plastic Inevitable: Sod in Heaven by MPTL Microplastics
item.description: Many membered South London group drag the capital's post-punk scene into strange and scrappy new realms, restlessly documenting England’s deep reserves of residual weirdness

item.title: Baroque and Roll: Halt by Foetus
item.description: After 45 years, JG Thirlwell has decided to bring his ever-evolving Foetus project to a close with an album that reflects on the past, while casting an uneasy eye on the present. Wesley Doyle waves goodbye

item.title: Wrigglies Extra: Unite The Worms by Golden Toad
item.description: Japanese Television's Al Brown eagerly licks the toxins off the back of a Sonoran desert toad and sinks into a bottomless funk of motorik rhythms, twisting basslines and Balearic guitars resulting in an album of slithery bedroom electro-psych well-suited to the late, winter months

item.title: Laser Focussed: FRKWYS Vol. 18: Extended Field by Arnold Dreyblatt & Horse Lords
item.description: Team-up between the legendary minimalist composer and double-bassist with the kickass Baltimore freak rockers feels like a true meeting of minds, finds Levi Dayan

item.title: Who Says Free Music Can’t Dance? Sama’a (Audition) by [Ahmed]
item.description: With one of the great rhythm sections of our time, the free jazz quartet take the pan-Islamic music of Ahmed Abdul-Malik to far out places – without sparing an ounce of its propulsive energy

item.title: A Topography of Intimacy: A Body Like A Home by Alejandra Cárdenas
item.description: To the sound of cumbia rhythms, noisy bass tremors, frantic violin phrases and spectral electronics, the Peruvian-born artist creates a moving meditation on home and homeliness, from the unconscious desires buried in the basement to the hopes and dreams spiralling from the attic room

item.title: Chopper Choons: Mercy by Armand Hammer and The Alchemist
item.description: On their seventh stellar album in a row, the unbeatable duo of Elucid and Billy Woods find space for small, everyday joys amongst the horror of contemporary geopolitics

item.title: Heavy Weather: A Theory of Cloud by Shrine Maiden
item.description: Husband and wife duo Rachel Nakawatase and Ryan Betschart blend their doom with dreampop, noise, ambient and shoegaze elements, exploding the confines of linear perspective into new forms of sonic space
